message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** General research on SPEED LISTENING for a SIGHTED person, focused on IMPROVEMENT methods and realistic limits. Address all five: (1) Best evidence-based TRAINING methods/protocols for a sighted person to increase speed-listening comprehension — progressive ramp/overload schedules, daily practice duration, adaptation techniques, plateau-breaking, and how long measurable gains take. (2) How fast can a SIGHTED (non-blind) person realistically get with dedicated training — is there ANY documented evidence of sighted listeners reaching the 16-22 syllables/second range that trained late-blind listeners achieve, and what is the practical sighted ceiling in syllables/second AND words/minute? (3) Techniques to increase information THROUGHPUT WITHOUT raising syllable rate — silence/pause trimming, content-aware/variable-rate compression, optimal chunking, and inserting silent gaps to drain the phonological-storage buffer (and optimal gap timing/ratio). (4) Tools/apps/players that exceed Audible's 3.5x cap (e.g. custom players, Rubber Band/SoundTouch re-encoding, screen-reader TTS) and support these techniques. (5) Practical community/practitioner knowledge from speed-listening enthusiasts and blind/screen-reader power users. CONTEXT: the user comprehends Audible at 3.5x sustained over 4 months (Audible '1x' is slow narration ~150 wpm, so 3.5x ≈ ~13 syllables/second). They want the equivalent of '10x' ideally, '7x' acceptable. IMPORTANT: translate these 'x' multipliers into syllables/second and words/minute against BOTH the slow-narration baseline AND a normal conversational baseline, and assess honestly whether 7x and 10x are physically achievable given documented human comprehension ceilings (~22 syll/sec trained blind, ~11-16 syll/sec untrained sighted). Avoid re-deriving basic TSM/WSOLA/PSOLA algorithm internals (already covered in prior research) — focus on training, the sighted ceiling, throughput tricks, tools, and practitioner methods.

20 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Trained late-blind listeners can comprehend accelerated (time-compressed) speech at up to approximately 22 syllables per second, whereas normal sighted listeners' upper limit is about 8 syllables per second.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (primary)
Quote: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly supported, near-verbatim, by the quoted text in the primary peer-reviewed source: Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann (2013), Frontiers in Human Neuroscience (PMC3805979), which states blind individuals "may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second...exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." The 22-vs-8 pairing originates from Moos & Trouvain (2007), a real cited peer-reviewed ICPhS study, not a misread or marketing source. Independently corroborated: the same group's fMRI study (PMC3847124 / BMC Neuroscience 2013) reproduces the framing, and Scientific American reports 25 syl/s (blind) vs ~10 syl/s max (sighted) — same order of magnitude, not contradicting. No credible source disputes the directional finding. Minor qualifications that do NOT refute: (1) the 22 syl/s figure is cited from Moos & Trouvain rather than the authors' own measurement (their own training study reached ~18 syl/s at ~70% comprehension); (2) "8 syl/s" is the top of a stated 4-8 syl/s sighted range, with other sources putting the practical sighted ceiling near 10 syl/s. The claim accurately reports the source and the central comparison holds across multiple independent peer-reviewed studies.

### [1] Trained late-blind/daily-synthesis-user listeners reached the upper ceiling: some blind subjects still understood formant-synthesized speech at 22 syllables/second, confirming ~22 s/s as the documented upper bound for trained listeners and that this skill is tied to years of daily training, not available to untrained sighted listeners.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/COMPREHENSION-OF-ULTRA-FAST-SPEECH-BLIND-VS.-Moos-Trouvain/d403dd5301eaff3c66d6ed3f015de47aee66e02f (primary)
Quote: "The fact that some of the blind still understood speech at 22 s/s reveals the enormous flexibility of the brain in speech perception during the processing of ultra-fast speech."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by primary and multiple corroborating peer-reviewed sources. The supporting quote ("some of the blind still understood speech at 22 s/s") directly backs the core factual claim. Moos & Trouvain (2007/2008) is the primary conference paper. Independently corroborated by Frontiers Hum. Neurosci. 2013 (PMC3805979), which states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s (Moos and Trouvain, 2007)." Multiple fMRI studies (BMC Neuroscience 2013, PMC3847124) confirm the same figures and tie the skill to late-blind training/cross-modal plasticity. Formant synthesis (JAWS screen reader) was the actual stimulus type used, so "formant-synthesized" is accurate. The point most relevant to the user — that ~22 s/s is NOT available to untrained sighted listeners (sighted ceiling ~8-10 syl/s) — is unanimous across all sources. CAVEATS (nuances, not refutations): (a) minor figure variance — Scientific American popular-press piece cites "up to 25 syl/s" blind / "10 syl/s" sighted, while the academic literature uses ~22 / ~8; the claim matches the primary academic source. (b) The "years of daily training, not innate" framing is slightly oversimplified: literature attributes the ability to a combination of daily screen-reader use AND blindness-driven recruitment of visual cortex (late-blind training studies show de novo V1 activation; only 4 of 6 trainees improved), so it is training-plus-plasticity, not training alone. These do not undermine the operative claim. Source quality (primary peer-reviewed, repeatedly replicated 2007-2018) matches claim strength; not outdated, not marketing.

### [2] The blind listeners' extreme advantage existed only for formant-synthesized speech, NOT for compressed natural speech; for natural speech the blind scored only slightly better than sighted, and the ability to comprehend ultra-fast synthetic speech did not transfer to ultra-fast compressed natural speech. This means the 22 s/s ceiling is achieved with hyper-articulated synthetic TTS, not with compressed audiobook narration — relevant to the user's tool choice (screen-reader TTS vs. re-encoded Audible audio).
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/COMPREHENSION-OF-ULTRA-FAST-SPEECH-BLIND-VS.-Moos-Trouvain/d403dd5301eaff3c66d6ed3f015de47aee66e02f (primary)
Quote: "the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source AND independently corroborated, so it survives refutation. (1) PRIMARY SOURCE: Moos & Trouvain (2007), ICPhS proceedings — a legitimate peer-reviewed conference paper from established speech scientists. It tested blind listeners at 17-22 syll/sec vs sighted at 9-14 syll/sec and found formant synthesis specifically advantaged at high rates; the supporting quote ('the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes') is consistent with the study's design and conclusion. (2) INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION #1 — the 2013 Frontiers/PMC training+fMRI study (PMC3805979) used EXCLUSIVELY formant-synthesized JAWS 'Eloquence' TTS for all training and reached only ~70% comprehension at 18 syll/sec; it provides no evidence that trained blind listeners hit ~22 syll/sec with natural compressed speech. This confirms the documented extreme rates are a synthetic-TTS phenomenon, not compressed-audiobook narration. (3) INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION #2 — multiple sources confirm blind listeners specifically prefer/perform better with the LESS natural formant synthesis over diphone/natural speech at very fast rates. (4) STRONGEST CORROBORATION — the 2018 Journal of Speech Sciences study (Trouvain et al.) on time-compressed NATURAL utterances (9-19 syll/sec) found sighted subjects actually scored BETTER on intelligibility and blind only slightly better on acceptability, a result the authors explicitly note 'contradicts a trend often reported in the literature' — directly supporting that the blind advantage collapses for natural compressed speech. (5) Sources are peer-reviewed academic (not marketing/forum), span 2007-2018 in a stable psychoacoustics domain (not outdated). MINOR CAVEAT (does not refute): the 2008 fMRI single-case (Moos et al., amlap.org) found one exceptional blind subject who DID understand ultra-fast NATURAL speech at 16 syll/sec — so 'no transfer at all' is slightly overstated for elite individuals, and natural recognition was still lower than synthetic. But the core practical claim — that the 22 syll/sec ceiling is achieved with hyper-articulated synthetic TTS rather than compressed audiobook narration, and that the blind/sighted gap largely vanishes for natural speech — is confirmed by independent primary sources and is directly relevant to the user's screen-reader-TTS-vs-re-encoded-Audible tool choice.

### [3] Speech time-compressed by 3x (syllable rhythm 3x faster than original) has poor intelligibility, scoring below 50% words correct, but inserting silent gaps restores intelligibility.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19390234/ (primary)
Quote: "The TTS waveform was time-compressed by a factor of 3, creating a signal with a syllable rhythm three times faster than the original, and whose intelligibility is poor (<50% words correct)."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED AGAINST PRIMARY SOURCE. The claim quotes Ghitza & Greenberg (2009), "On the possible role of brain rhythms in speech perception," Phonetica 66(1-2):113-126 (PMID 19390234). The supporting quote is verbatim from the published abstract (confirmed via PubMed and Karger): TTS waveform time-compressed by factor 3 yields a syllable rhythm 3x faster "whose intelligibility is poor (<50% words correct)," and inserting silences restores it (lowest word error rate with 80 ms silence inserted periodically). Source quality is excellent: peer-reviewed, foundational, highly cited in the theta-oscillation/speech-rhythm literature.

CONTRADICTION CHECK: Ghitza (2014, PMC4082093) Experiment I reports ~96% accuracy at compression factor 3, which superficially conflicts with "<50%." BUT this is a STIMULUS-CONSTRUCTION difference, not a refutation: the 2009 study used TTS material chopped into uniform 40-ms fragments, whereas 2014 Exp I used naturally-segmented uncompressed speech preserving natural syllabic packaging. Crucially, BOTH papers explicitly confirm the load-bearing part of the claim — that inserting silent gaps ("repackaging") substantially RESTORES intelligibility of severely compressed speech (2014: "intelligibility was substantially restored when the information stream was re-packaged by inserting gaps"; optimal packaging rate ~9 Hz / theta band). Independent corroboration: Nature Sci Reports s41598-023-29755-x, J Neuroscience 37(33):7930. Replicated/extended through 2014-2023, so not outdated; not marketing.

QUALIFICATION for synthesis: the exact "<50% words correct at factor 3" number is condition-specific (TTS + 40-ms uniform fragments) and another careful study got 96% at factor 3 under different segmentation. The robust, transferable takeaway for the user is the gap-insertion/repackaging mechanism itself: optimal ~80 ms periodic silent gaps restoring a ~9 Hz (high-theta) syllabic packaging rate improves comprehension of heavily time-compressed speech. The directional claim (raw 3x compression hurts intelligibility; inserted silent gaps restore it) is well-supported.

### [4] Inserting silence into 3x-compressed speech yields a U-shaped word-error-rate curve, with the lowest error rate (highest intelligibility) achieved when silent gaps of 80 ms are inserted PERIODICALLY; the optimal silence duration is 80 ms.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19390234/ (primary)
Quote: "The performance curve (word error rate as a function of mean duration of silence) was U-shaped. The lowest word error rate (i.e., highest intelligibility) occurred when the silence was 80 ms long and inserted periodically."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source: Ghitza & Greenberg (2009), "On the possible role of brain rhythms in speech perception: intelligibility of time-compressed speech with periodic and aperiodic insertions of silence," Phonetica 66(1-2):113-126 (PMID 19390234). Verified via WebFetch of the PubMed abstract and corroborated by two independent secondary searches (Karger/Phonetica, ResearchGate figure, multiple citing PMC papers). The study: TTS sentences were time-compressed by factor 3 (syllable rhythm 3x faster, <50% words correct intact), then chopped into consecutive 40-ms acoustic fragments each followed by an inserted silent gap of variable length (0-160 ms), inserted either periodically or aperiodically. Verbatim finding: "The performance curve (word error rate as a function of mean duration of silence) was U-shaped. The lowest word error rate (i.e., highest intelligibility) occurred when the silence was 80 ms long and inserted periodically." Intelligibility was restored as long as gaps were 20-120 ms; aperiodic insertion at 80 ms raised error rate. No contradicting source found; the finding is the foundational empirical result behind Ghitza's TEMPO/theta-oscillation framework and is repeatedly cited/replicated (e.g., JASA 2018 aging study, Frontiers 2014). Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study) matches claim strength. One caveat that does NOT refute the claim: the 80-ms optimum is specific to the 40-ms-fragment + 3x paradigm and to theta-band re-synchronization, so it is not a universal "insert 80 ms anywhere" recipe -- but the claim as worded ("lowest error when 80 ms gaps inserted periodically into 3x-compressed speech") is precisely what the study reports.

### [5] Periodic (regularly-timed) insertion of silent gaps is superior to aperiodic (random) insertion for recovering intelligibility of compressed speech; aperiodic insertion increases word error rate.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19390234/ (primary)
Quote: "word error rate increased when the silence was inserted aperiodically."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and faithfully supported by the primary source (Ghitza & Greenberg 2009, "On the possible role of brain rhythms in speech perception," Phonetica 66(1-2):113-26, PMID 19390234). The abstract states the lowest word error rate (highest intelligibility) occurred with 80ms silence inserted PERIODICALLY, and explicitly: "This is also the condition for which word error rate increased when the silence was inserted aperiodically." This is peer-reviewed primary experimental research by established researchers, matching the claim's strength. I specifically searched for replication failures/critiques and found NONE; instead, multiple independent later sources corroborate it — Nature Scientific Reports (2023, 2025), bioRxiv (2025), and Frontiers reviews all cite that "results indicated better overall performance with periodic than with aperiodic silence rates." The finding remains a cited reference point through 2025, so it is not outdated. The claim is an accurate paraphrase, not an overreach. CAVEAT (refines, does not refute): the finding is specific — speech was compressed 3x (beyond comprehension), 40ms fragments, and the periodic advantage is clearest at the optimal ~80ms gap (restoring theta-band 6-12 Hz rhythm); it is not a blanket rule that any aperiodic gap pattern always hurts.

### [6] Training on time-compressed speech that begins with mild distortion (a progressive ramp from easy to hard) produces better learning than training that presents severe compression from the start; the constant-high (30% duration) group performed worst of all trained groups, while an adaptive-incremental ramp reached 91% word accuracy at the same 30% compression level where the constant-high group reached only 76%.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5436740/ (primary)
Quote: "Training procedures that started off with relatively little signal distortion were advantageous compared to...severe distortions presented from the very beginning. ... adaptive-incremental achieved greater accuracy (M = .91) versus constant-high (M = .76)."
Verifier evidence (high): All three factual components of the claim are directly and exactly verified against the primary source (Cohen et al., "The perceptual learning of time-compressed speech: A comparison of training protocols with different levels of difficulty," PLoS One 2017, 12(5):e0176488 = PMC5436740). (1) Progressive ramp beats severe-from-start: the abstract states "training procedures that start off with relatively little signal distortion ('easy' items...) may be advantageous compared to conditions wherein severe distortions are presented...from the very beginning." (2) Constant-high (30% duration) worst of trained groups: confirmed — "training in the constant-high compression condition resulted in significantly smaller gains also compared to the two adaptive training conditions," smallest gains on both trained and novel items, no trained group performed worse. (3) The 91% vs 76% figures match exactly: at the 4th training block where compression was equal (30%) in both groups, adaptive-incremental M=.91 (SE=.02) vs constant-high M=.76 (SE=.02), confirmed in Figure 4. Population was 65 normal-hearing sighted Hebrew-speaking adults (18-35) — directly relevant to the sighted-listener research question, not blind subjects. Source quality is strong: peer-reviewed primary RCT-style study, corroborated by independent WebSearch and PubMed (28545039). MINOR caveat (does not refute): the claim's "produces better learning" is slightly firmer than the study's hedged "may be advantageous," and the claim omits the documented trade-off that adaptive protocols better support generalization to untrained tokens while authors noted "the lack of a clearly advantageous training protocol...may reflect the specific conditions of the current design rather than a general rule." This is mild over-tightening of hedged language, not a misread — the load-bearing quantitative and ordinal facts are accurate.

### [7] Measurable perceptual-learning gains for time-compressed speech occur within a single ~1-hour session of training (5 blocks of 60 trials, 300 total), after which trained listeners identify compressed speech significantly more accurately than minimally-exposed controls.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5436740/ (primary)
Quote: "The experiment was administered in one session of approximately one hour. ... After training, recognition of the trained materials was significantly more accurate in trained listeners than in listeners who received only brief exposure to time-compressed speech."
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source CONFIRMS the claim directly. PMC5436740 = Gabay, Karni & Banai (2017), "The perceptual learning of time-compressed speech: A comparison of training protocols with different levels of difficulty." Methods: a single session of ~1 hour, 5 blocks of 60 trials = 300 total trials, exactly as the claim states. Results: "all four protocols resulted in significant post-training improvement"; all four trained groups significantly outperformed the untrained control group on the repeated/trained condition (p < .01). The control group "participated in pre-test and post-test phases only" (minimal exposure, no training) — matching the claim's "minimally-exposed controls." The supporting quote accurately paraphrases the paper. CORROBORATING (not contradicting) literature: Banai & Lavner (PMC3467231, "More than Rapid Adaptation") and Adank & Janse / Sebastian-Galles work (PMC2775905) show adaptation to time-compressed speech occurs even FASTER — significant improvement within just 10-20 sentences (first block of 16), well inside a single session. No source disputes within-session learning; the field uniformly reports rapid early gains. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary psycholinguistic research, 2017, replicated finding) matches the modest, non-extraordinary claim. Minor caveat: post-training gains in the 2017 study were partly token/stimulus-specific and protocol-dependent (constant-low > constant-high), but this does not undercut the claim's core assertion (within-session gains; trained > minimally-exposed controls). Claim is well-supported, current, not marketing, and not outdated.

### [8] Perceptual learning of time-compressed speech progresses in two phases: an initial rapid/general phase followed by a prolonged, more stimulus-specific phase — meaning gains require multiple sessions rather than a single sitting, with most improvement occurring between sessions 1 and 2.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (primary)
Quote: "learning of time-compressed speech progresses through an initial general phase and a subsequent prolonged and more stimulus-specific phase"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is fully supported by the primary source (Banai & Lavner 2012, PLOS One, "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation," PMC3467231). The paper states verbatim: "the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech appears to progress from an initial, rapid adaptation phase to a subsequent prolonged and more stimulus specific phase" — matching the supporting quote and the two-phase claim. The sub-claim that gains require multiple sessions with most improvement between sessions 1 and 2 is also directly supported: the paper reports "thresholds improved significantly between the first two training sessions, with marginal improvements on the two subsequent sessions" and "at the group level, learning asymptotes after two training sessions." Multi-session training produced more learning AND generalization to novel materials than single-session/brief exposure. Adversarial searches for contradicting evidence returned zero disputes — only corroborating peer-reviewed work, including a 2017 follow-up (PMC5436740) and a 2022 study (PubMed 35705680) that extend rather than refute the two-phase framework (interpreted via Reverse Hierarchy Theory). Source is a peer-reviewed empirical study, not marketing/benchmark/forum content. The 2012 date is acceptable for this slow-moving, foundational psychophysics subfield where the finding has been built upon, not overturned. No overreach: the claim's wording ("initial rapid/general phase" then "prolonged stimulus-specific phase") accurately reflects the paper.

### [9] A structured training protocol of 5 sessions over 10 days (300 sentences per session) using an adaptive two-down one-up staircase produced large, measurable comprehension gains (Cohen's d = 1.37), establishing a concrete evidence-based ramp/overload schedule for improving time-compressed speech comprehension.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (primary)
Quote: "Five sessions were administered over the course of 10 days. During each session, listeners had to verify sentences taken from the training set"
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against primary source (Banai & Lavner 2012, PLOS ONE, PMC3467231). All concrete protocol numbers are accurate: the paper states "Five sessions were administered over the course of 10 days" and each session used "5 adaptive blocks of 60 trials" (= 300 sentences/session), with "A two-down one-up procedure ... with an initial compression level of 65%." Cohen's d = 1.37 appears in Table 2 for the TRAINED group's pre/post difference scores (0.24±0.2). The protocol is correctly described and the source is a peer-reviewed primary study — sufficient quality. CAVEATS (qualifications, not refutations): (1) The study measured SENTENCE VERIFICATION THRESHOLDS (semantic-plausibility judgments tracking the compression level at which performance stabilized), not comprehension accuracy/recall/wpm — so calling d=1.37 a "comprehension gain" is loose interpretive framing. (2) The claim cherry-picks the larger effect size: within-training improvement (first vs last session) was d=0.56 ("intermediate effect size"); d=1.37 is the pre/post trained-condition difference. (3) The claim omits the paper's central finding that transfer to UNTRAINED conditions was limited. (4) Participants were normal-hearing university students (non-native Hebrew/Arabic speakers), effectively sighted, consistent with the research question. Net: the load-bearing factual scaffolding (5 sessions/10 days/300 sentences/adaptive staircase/d=1.37) is correct and well-sourced; only the "comprehension" gloss and selective effect-size choice are overstated. Not enough to refute.

### [10] Brief exposure to time-compressed speech (just 20 sentences) produces measurable perceptual learning, but this rapid learning only improves comprehension of the specific trained material and does NOT generalize to untrained speech tokens.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25324090/ (primary)
Quote: "Brief exposure to time-compressed speech yields both learning and generalization... Trained listeners outperformed the other groups of listeners on the trained condition, but only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by the primary source and independently corroborated. The source is Banai & Lavner (2014), "The effects of training length on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its generalization," JASA 136(4):1908 (PMID 25324090) — a peer-reviewed primary study, appropriate quality for the claim's strength. The abstract confirms exactly the claim: a brief-exposure group (20 sentences) and a single-session group improved on TRAINED material, but "only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens." So brief exposure produced measurable learning that did NOT generalize to untrained tokens — precisely the claim. The supporting quote's opening "yields both learning and generalization" is the paper's overall framing (generalization came from the 3-session group), and the claim correctly carves out that BRIEF exposure specifically does not generalize — no overreach. A SECOND independent paper from the same group (Banai et al., "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation," PLOS ONE 2012, PMC3467231) replicates the finding: "Learning from brief exposure was specific and did not generalize to stimuli not encountered during the initial exposure phase," whereas multiday training generalized across talkers. The 2019 Springer follow-up (s13414-019-01714-7) and 2017 PLOS ONE protocol paper are consistent. Not outdated (stable cognitive-psychology result), not marketing, not a press release or forum speculation. Practical implication for the user's question is sound: rapid gains from short exposure are stimulus-specific, so durable, transferable speed-listening improvement requires sustained multi-session training — consistent with the user's reported 4-month adaptation arc.

### [11] Trained late-blind individuals can comprehend accelerated speech at rates up to approximately 22 syllables per second.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full (primary)
Quote: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated independently. The Frontiers article (Dietrich et al. 2013, fMRI training study of late-blind subjects) states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." This traces to the original Moos & Trouvain (2007, ICPhS) experiment ("vision loss of peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second"). Independent corroboration: BMC Neuroscience 2013 (PMC3847124) and PubMed both report blind listeners comprehending up to 22 syll/s vs ~8 syll/s for sighted. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary research) matches the claim strength; not outdated; not marketing. Minor caveat: the "22 syll/s" figure is an upper bound for trained/proficient late-blind listeners (the study's expert group), not a universal late-blind capacity — but the claim's wording ("up to approximately 22") already encodes this correctly. No contradicting source found.

### [12] After approximately 6 months of training, four of six late-blind participants improved from below 14% to about 70% comprehension accuracy at 18 syllables per second.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full (primary)
Quote: "four subjects (nos. 147, 150, 144, and 142) showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points"
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary source (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann 2013, Front. Hum. Neurosci., PMC3805979). The core quantitative claim is accurate: (a) Training ~6 months — "ca. 6 months." (b) Baseline <14% — "the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances" at 18 syl/s. (c) Four of six improved to ~70% — "Four of six participants showed... a significant improvement of ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech comprehension, in terms of a performance level of ca. 70% points" / "four subjects (nos. 147, 150, 144, and 142) showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points." (d) Rate 18 syl/s — confirmed. Source is a primary peer-reviewed study; strength matches. ONE factual error in the claim: it says "four of six LATE-BLIND participants," but the study had FIVE blind subjects plus ONE normal-sighted individual (six total), and subject 142 — one of the four improvers — was the normal-sighted control (post-training ~67% at 18 syl/s). So the four improvers were three late-blind + one sighted, NOT four late-blind. This mischaracterizes participant composition but does NOT contradict the substantive numbers (4/6, <14%->~70%, 18 syl/s, ~6 mo), which are all correct. Note: "70% points" = absolute percentage-point performance level (~70% correct), not a 70-point gain on top of 70%. The misattribution is a minor overreach, not grounds to kill the claim; if anything the sighted improver (142) makes this MORE relevant to the research question's sighted-ceiling inquiry. Not outdated (2013 remains the definitive primary study on trained ultra-fast listening; not a fast-moving field). Not marketing/forum. Recommend the synthesis correct "late-blind" to "blind-and-sighted (five blind + one sighted; one sighted subject among the four improvers)."

### [13] Inserting silent pauses at clause and sentence boundaries into time-compressed speech ('time-restoration') restored recall accuracy of normal-hearing young adults to a level statistically indistinguishable from normal-rate speech (~95% recovery, p=.874), even though the words were still delivered at the fast compressed syllable rate.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/23312165231203514 (primary)
Quote: "Experiment 1 was conducted with 27 normal-hearing young adults as a proof-of-concept demonstration that restoring lost processing time by inserting silent pauses at linguistically salient points within a time-compressed narrative ("time-restoration") returns recall accuracy to a level approximating that for a normal speech rate."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary source full text (Trends in Hearing 2023, O'Leary, Neukam, Hansen, Kinney, Capach, Svirsky, Wingfield; PMC10637151, peer-reviewed, CC-BY-NC). Experiment 1 used 27 normal-hearing young adults. The full text states verbatim: time-restoration "raised the level of recall accuracy for otherwise time-compressed speech 95% of the way back to baseline, a level that did not differ significantly from the accuracy level for passages presented with their original normal (N) speech rate (p = .874)." Every claim component checks out exactly: ~95% recovery (CONFIRMED), p=.874 (CONFIRMED), statistically indistinguishable from normal-rate speech (CONFIRMED — "did not differ significantly"), pauses inserted at clause/sentence boundaries (CONFIRMED), and words still at fast compressed syllable rate (CONFIRMED — "the speech itself remained time-compressed... the improved recall can be attributed to allowing listeners' processing additional time to 'catch up' with the input rather than affecting the clarity of the signal itself"). Time-compression alone significantly reduced recall (p<.001 in all clarity conditions), confirming the deficit that restoration reversed. IMPORTANT SCOPE CAVEAT (qualification, not refutation): the p=.874/95% figure is specifically for the CLEAR (unvocoded) speech condition; the benefit weakened under noise-vocoder degradation (10-channel: 76% recovery p=.002; 6-channel: 31% p=.005). The claim correctly scopes itself to normal-hearing young adults / clear-speech proof-of-concept and does NOT overgeneralize, so this does not contradict it. No credible contradicting source found; corroborated by Wingfield's prior 1999 "Regaining lost time" work. Source quality matches claim strength; current (2023); not marketing/cherry-picked. Claim is well-supported, current, and primary-sourced.

### [14] The optimal gap-insertion scheme placed silence only at linguistic boundaries (not uniformly), with sentence-boundary pauses set at twice the duration of clause-boundary pauses, and total inserted silence calibrated so the time-restored passage matched the duration of the original uncompressed recording.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/23312165231203514 (primary)
Quote: "the durations of the silent periods added at sentence boundaries were set at twice the duration of the silent periods added at clause boundaries ... the total duration of the silent periods was set such that the duration of the time-restored compressed passages was equal to the duration of the original un-compressed recordings"
Verifier evidence (high): All three substantive components of the claim are verbatim-confirmed in the primary source full text (O'Leary, Neukam, Hansen, Kinney, Capach, Svirsky, Wingfield, "Strategic Pauses Relieve Listeners from the Effort of Listening to Fast Speech," Trends in Hearing 2023, DOI 10.1177/23312165231203514, open-access PMC10637151). Retrieved Methods text states verbatim: (1) "the durations of the silent periods added at sentence boundaries were set at twice the duration of the silent periods added at clause boundaries"; (2) "the total duration of the silent periods was set such that the duration of the time-restored compressed passages was equal to the duration of the original un-compressed recordings"; (3) silence was inserted "at clause and sentence boundaries... following prior studies showing that pauses at linguistically salient points are more effective in aiding recall than placing pauses at random points" — confirming placement at linguistic boundaries, not uniformly. Source is current (2023), peer-reviewed primary research in a reputable SAGE journal, not marketing/forum/benchmark. Independent web searches corroborated the same details. ONE minor caveat: the claim's word "optimal" is the claim-author's characterization — this specific study did NOT run a head-to-head uniform-vs-boundary comparison; it adopted boundary placement from prior literature. This softness in framing does not undermine the three quantitative facts, which are exactly correct. (Note also: study population is cochlear-implant and normal-hearing listeners, not the sighted/blind speed-listening framing of the research question, but the claim only describes the gap scheme, which is reported accurately.)

### [15] The deficit from fast speech is attributed to loss of cognitive processing time rather than perceptual intelligibility, and restoring that time via pauses reduced listening effort as measured by pupil dilation.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/23312165231203514 (primary)
Quote: "the rapidity of time-compressed speech can deprive the listener of the ordinarily available processing time present when speech is delivered at a normal speech rate ... Pupil dilation indicated that additional effort was expended by participants while attempting to process the time-compressed narratives, with the effortful demand on resources reduced with time restoration."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED, not refuted. Source is a genuine peer-reviewed primary paper: O'Leary, Neukam, Hansen, Kinney, Capach, Svirsky & Wingfield (2023), "Strategic Pauses Relieve Listeners from the Effort of Listening to Fast Speech: Data Limited and Resource Limited Processes in Narrative Recall by Adult Users of Cochlear Implants," Trends in Hearing (SAGE), DOI 10.1177/23312165231203514, PubMed 37941344. Wingfield is an established speech/aging/auditory cognition researcher.

(1) Claim supported by quote: YES. The abstract states "the rapidity of time-compressed speech can deprive the listener of the ordinarily available processing time present when speech is delivered at a normal speech rate" and "Pupil dilation indicated that additional effort was expended by participants while attempting to process the time-compressed narratives, with the effortful demand on resources reduced with time restoration." Experiment 1 (27 normal-hearing young adults) was an explicit proof-of-concept that inserting silent pauses at linguistically salient points within a time-compressed narrative returns recall accuracy to near-normal-rate levels, with pupillometry showing reduced effort. This directly supports both halves of the claim.

(2) Contradicting evidence: none found. Multiple corroborating/converging papers exist (Nature Sci Reports on syllabic-rhythm restoration in time-compressed speech; Frontiers self-pacing of vocoded discourse; earlier work that inserting silent pauses improves recall/comprehension). No credible source disputes the core mechanism.

(3) Source quality matches claim strength: YES — primary, peer-reviewed, with both physiological (pupillometry) and behavioral (recall) measures.

(4) Not outdated (2023).

(5) Not marketing/forum/press-release.

ONE QUALIFICATION (does not refute): The claim's strong phrasing "rather than perceptual intelligibility" is cleanest for the NORMAL-HEARING time-compression case (Exp 1). The paper's actual framing distinguishes "data-limited" (perceptual/sensory degradation) from "resource-limited" (cognitive processing time); for cochlear-implant users (Exp 2) perceptual/data-limited factors ALSO contribute and time-restoration only helped a subgroup with better working memory and word/sentence recognition. So the deficit is NOT exclusively processing-time across all populations — but for sustained-fast-speech in non-impaired (relevant to a sighted listener), the processing-time/resource-limited account and the pause-restoration benefit are well-supported. Net: claim is well-supported and should be presented with the data-limited-vs-resource-limited nuance.

### [16] Normal-hearing young adults' intelligibility collapses at high syllable rates: 99.8% at the control rate of 6.6 sps versus only 41.9% at 15.6 sps — quantifying the steep comprehension ceiling for untrained sighted listeners well below the 16-22 sps range trained blind listeners achieve.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9938863/ (primary)
Quote: "the average intelligibility across listeners was significantly reduced to 41.9% (SD = 18.0%, range = 12.5–84.4%)"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source PMC9938863 (peer-reviewed, 2023) verbatim confirms both numbers: control 6.6 sps = "average intelligibility ... of 99.8% (SD = 0.8%)"; fastest 15.6 sps = "significantly reduced ... to 41.9% (SD = 18.0%, range = 12.5–84.4%)". Listeners were 14 normal-hearing young adults (mean 22.3 yrs), untrained, single 1.5-2h session, time-compressed natural speech (LIST corpus). This matches the claim's framing of an untrained-listener ceiling. The 16-22 sps trained-blind comparison is independently corroborated by the literature (Hertrich/Ackermann ultra-fast-speech work; blind listeners comprehending up to 22 sps after training, >16 sps for TTS reading), so the contrast is real, not invented. Caveats that fall short of refutation: (a) the study calls participants "normal-hearing young adults," not "sighted" — the sighted label is the claim author's defensible overlay; (b) only TWO rates were tested (no intermediate curve), and 15.6 sps was deliberately selected as the ~50% speech-reception threshold, so "collapses" is mild rhetorical editorializing rather than an observed sudden drop. But the substantive quantitative claim (≈100% → ≈42% intelligibility from 6.6 to 15.6 sps) is accurate, correctly quoted, current, and from an appropriate primary source. Contradicting search hit (Oliveira et al., sighted > blind on intelligibility) does not undermine the claim; it reinforces that untrained sighted listeners hit a steep ceiling and that 16-22 sps is a trained-blind feat.

### [17] The speech-speed Chrome extension implements content-aware variable-rate playback that normalizes speech to a constant target syllable rate (default 9 syllables/second) by speeding up slow speakers more and fast speakers less, rather than applying a single fixed multiplier.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/ywong137/speech-speed (primary)
Quote: "The goal is to normalize all speech to a comfortable listening rate ... the default target of 9 syl/s. Slow speakers get sped up more; fast speakers get sped up less."
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED by primary source (github.com/ywong137/speech-speed README). All claim elements verified verbatim and via independent re-fetch: (1) Content-aware variable-rate playback — extension captures audio from the page's video element, isolates 2-10 Hz syllable-rate modulation of the energy envelope via high-pass filter, and counts zero-crossings to estimate the speaker's natural rate. (2) Normalizes to a constant target — README: "The goal is to normalize all speech to a comfortable listening rate." (3) Default 9 syl/s — README: "Target rate (4-14 syl/s, default 9): the desired effective syllable rate you hear." (4) Inverse adjustment, not fixed multiplier — README: "Slow speakers get sped up more; fast speakers get sped up less," with worked examples (~3.0 syl/s -> 3.00x, ~4.5 syl/s -> 2.00x, ~6.0 syl/s -> 1.50x). Corroborated by third-party coverage (TechPP, March 2026: "auto-adjusts YouTube speed to match speech") and a Hacker News thread (item 47361251). Source quality matches claim strength (primary repo for a factual description of its own behavior); claim is current (2026). The supporting quote in the claim matches the README exactly. Minor non-refuting caveat: the extension's own default Max speed cap is 3.5x ("Max speed 1.5-5x, default 3.5"), so it does not by itself exceed Audible's 3.5x ceiling — but the claim is solely about the normalization mechanism, which is accurate.

### [18] The extension's default maximum playback ceiling is 3.5x, configurable up to 5x, so it does not natively exceed Audible's 3.5x cap by default and only modestly exceeds it at the upper bound, indicating this tool is not a high-multiplier (7x-10x) solution.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://github.com/ywong137/speech-speed (primary)
Quote: "Max speed (1.5-5x, default 3.5): ceiling for playback rate. Protects against extreme speedup on very slow speakers."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported by the primary source (github.com/ywong137/speech-speed). The repo's Configuration section states verbatim: "Max speed (1.5-5x, default 3.5): ceiling for playback rate. Protects against extreme speedup on very slow speakers." This directly confirms all three factual assertions in the claim: (a) default max = 3.5x, (b) configurable upper bound = 5x, (c) therefore it does not natively exceed Audible's 3.5x by default and only reaches 5x at the user-set upper bound. Source quality is appropriate: this is a primary source (the tool's own docs) and the claim is a modest configuration fact, not an extraordinary claim. Currency confirmed — a TechPP article covering the extension is dated 2026-03-20 (recent). No contradicting evidence found; the only additional detail surfaced is that this is an AUTO-ADJUSTING extension (matches playback to speaker pace, docs note "some people can track 4-5x speed; others top out at 2x"), which reinforces rather than refutes the 5x ceiling. The claim's conclusion ("not a high-multiplier 7x-10x solution") is sound: 5x < the user's 7x-acceptable / 10x-ideal targets, and the claim is appropriately hedged ("only modestly exceeds at the upper bound"). One minor caveat not affecting the verdict: the 5x here is a YouTube-native-player multiplier against YouTube's conversational speech rate, not against Audible's slow ~150 wpm baseline, but the claim correctly references Audible's 3.5x cap for the comparison and the directional conclusion is unaffected.

### [19] Increased cortical tracking from inserted pauses does NOT directly establish improved comprehension/intelligibility; the relationship requires further investigation, so the buffer-drain/pause benefit for understanding is not confirmed by neural tracking alone.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0289288 (primary)
Quote: "Whether the increased cortical response...reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Deoisres et al., PLOS ONE, 2023-07-27, "Continuous speech with pauses inserted between words increases cortical tracking of speech envelope," DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0289288) verbatim supports the claim. Abstract: "Whether the increased cortical response, when longer pauses are introduced, reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation." Discussion reinforces: "the increase in cortical tracking when pauses were added to speech does not necessarily imply that the participants have improved speech understanding." The claim is a faithful paraphrase — and notably a CAUTIOUS/hedging claim asserting that the comprehension benefit is NOT confirmed by neural tracking alone, which the source explicitly states. Confirmed against both the PLOS ONE article and the PMC mirror (PMC10374040). Checklist: (1) quote matches verbatim, no overreach; (2) no contradicting source found — indeed the paper notes aggressive pause-insertion (166ms every 333ms) REDUCED intelligibility, strengthening the decoupling of tracking from comprehension; (3) peer-reviewed primary source, appropriate for a methodological-caution claim needing no extraordinary evidence; (4) current (2023); (5) not marketing/forum/cherry-pick — academic primary literature. Caveat: the claim correctly does NOT assert pauses help comprehension — it only states the buffer-drain/pause benefit is unproven by neural tracking, which is exactly what the source says. Cannot be refuted.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "The single normal-sighted control subject in the study reached a personal ceiling and reported that further training would not improve her ultra-fast speech perception, indicating a hard sighted limit well below the trained-blind range." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/, vote 0-3)
- "Sighted listeners without synthesis training comprehended compressed speech only up to the 9-14 syllables/second range, with comprehension declining continuously across that range; this is the practical sighted ceiling and corresponds to the most extreme rates of human speech production. This maps directly to the user's question: 3.5x of ~150 wpm slow narration is ~13 syll/sec, sitting at the very top of the documented untrained-sighted band, so the requested '7x' (~26 syll/sec) and '10x' (~37 syll/sec) lie far beyond any documented human comprehension and are not physically achievable." (https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/COMPREHENSION-OF-ULTRA-FAST-SPEECH-BLIND-VS.-Moos-Trouvain/d403dd5301eaff3c66d6ed3f015de47aee66e02f, vote 1-2)
- "Generalization of speed-listening skill to NEW, untrained material requires multi-session (three sessions) training; a single session is insufficient to produce transferable gains, indicating a progressive/longer training protocol is needed for real-world improvement." (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25324090/, vote 1-2)
- "The documented upper limit of comprehension for normal-sighted listeners is approximately 8 syllables per second, far below blind listeners' ceiling." (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full, vote 0-3)
- "Inserting 100ms silences between syllables of time-compressed speech (repackaging) to restore the syllabic rate to ~6.1 sps significantly improves intelligibility across all spectral conditions tested — a throughput technique that raises comprehension without lowering the underlying syllable production rate." (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9938863/, vote 1-2)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll synthesize the verified claims into a structured research report. All the work is done — these are pre-verified claims that I need to merge, group, and assess. Let me organize them into coherent findings.

input.summary: No — 7x and especially 10x are not physically achievable as raw syllable-rate increases for any human, sighted or blind. The user's current 3.5x of slow Audible narration (~150 wpm) is already ~13 syllables/second, sitting at the very top of the documented untrained-sighted comprehension band (~9-16 syll/sec, with intelligibility collapsing from ~100% at 6.6 sps to ~42% at 15.6 sps). The documented human ceiling is ~22 syll/sec, achieved ONLY by trained late-blind listeners using hyper-articulated formant-synthesized TTS (not compressed natural audiobook narration), and even that took ~6 months of daily training plus visual-cortex plasticity — and this advantage largely vanishes for natural compressed speech. A literal "7x" of the slow baseline would be ~26 syll/sec and "10x" ~37 syll/sec, both far beyond any documented human comprehension. The realistic path to higher effective throughput is NOT a higher multiplier but (a) sustained multi-session perceptual-learning training with a progressive easy-to-hard ramp to push from ~13 toward the ~15-18 syll/sec edge, and (b) throughput tricks that raise information density without raising syllable rate — chiefly trimming silence and inserting strategic silent gaps at clause/sentence boundaries ("time-restoration"), which restored normal-hearing recall to ~95% of normal-rate baseline even while words stayed time-compressed.

findings.claim: 7x and 10x are NOT physically achievable as raw syllable-rate multipliers. The user's 3.5x (~13 syll/sec) already sits at the top of the untrained-sighted band; the absolute human ceiling (~22 syll/sec, trained blind) corresponds to only ~6x of the slow Audible baseline and is unattainable by sighted listeners with compressed natural speech.
findings.evidence: Trained late-blind listeners reach up to ~22 syll/sec vs a normal sighted upper limit of ~8 syll/sec (Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann 2013, citing Moos & Trouvain 2007). Untrained normal-hearing adults' intelligibility collapses from 99.8% at 6.6 sps to 41.9% at 15.6 sps (PMC9938863, 2023). Translating the user's multipliers against the slow ~150 wpm / ~3.7 syll-sec-equivalent baseline: 3.5x ~= 13 syll/sec (already top of the 9-16 untrained band); 7x ~= ~26 syll/sec; 10x ~= ~37 syll/sec. Even the absolute documented ceiling of 22 syll/sec is only ~6x of the slow baseline, so 7x exceeds ALL documented human comprehension and 10x is far beyond it. The 22 syll/sec feat is also blind+synthetic-TTS-specific, not available for compressed audiobook narration. NOTE: two claims that pushed the strongest 'physically impossible' framing were split-voted (1-2) on technicalities of derivation, but the unanimous claims independently establish the same ceiling.

findings.claim: The documented ~16-22 syll/sec ceiling is achieved ONLY by trained late-blind listeners using hyper-articulated formant-synthesized TTS, NOT compressed natural audiobook narration; the blind advantage largely collapses for natural compressed speech, where sighted listeners perform comparably or even better. This directly bears on tool choice: screen-reader/formant TTS, not re-encoded Audible audio, is the medium where the highest rates are even theoretically reachable.
findings.evidence: Moos & Trouvain (2007): 'the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes.' The 2013 Frontiers training study used exclusively formant-synthesized JAWS 'Eloquence' TTS and reached only ~70% comprehension at 18 syll/sec. A 2018 Journal of Speech Sciences study on time-compressed NATURAL utterances (9-19 syll/sec) found sighted subjects scored BETTER on intelligibility and blind only slightly better on acceptability. One exceptional blind individual understood ultra-fast natural speech at 16 syll/sec, so transfer is not strictly zero for elite individuals, but the rule holds. Practical implication: the user's re-encoded Audible audio is natural speech, the medium where the blind/sighted gap nearly vanishes and the absolute ceiling is lower than the synthetic-TTS 22 syll/sec figure.

findings.claim: Best evidence-based training method: use a PROGRESSIVE easy-to-hard ramp (adaptive-incremental staircase), never severe compression from the start. A progressive ramp reached 91% word accuracy at 30% compression where a constant-high group reached only 76%; the constant-high (start-hard) group performed worst of all trained groups.
findings.evidence: Gabay/Karni/Banai (PLoS One 2017, PMC5436740), 65 normal-hearing sighted adults: 'Training procedures that started off with relatively little signal distortion were advantageous compared to severe distortions presented from the very beginning'; adaptive-incremental M=.91 vs constant-high M=.76 at the matched 30%-compression block. This is a concrete overload-scheduling recommendation directly relevant to a sighted listener: ramp difficulty gradually rather than jumping to maximum speed.

findings.claim: Concrete evidence-based training schedule and timeline: measurable gains appear within a single ~1-hour session (5 blocks of 60 trials = 300 items), but durable, transferable improvement requires multiple sessions. Learning proceeds in two phases — a rapid initial/general phase then a prolonged stimulus-specific phase — with most gain between sessions 1 and 2 and group-level asymptote after ~2 sessions. A documented protocol of 5 sessions over 10 days (300 sentences/session, two-down one-up adaptive staircase) produced a large effect (Cohen's d=1.37).
findings.evidence: PMC5436740: '~one hour... 5 blocks of 60 trials,' trained > minimally-exposed controls. PMC3467231 (Banai & Lavner 2012): two-phase learning, 'thresholds improved significantly between the first two training sessions... learning asymptotes after two training sessions,' 5 sessions/10 days/300 sentences, two-down one-up staircase from 65% compression, d=1.37 for trained pre/post. Caveat: d=1.37 is the pre/post trained-condition difference (within-training first-vs-last was d=0.56), and the measure was sentence-verification threshold, not wpm/recall. Practical ramp: short daily adaptive-staircase sessions, expect a fast initial bump then slower grind, plan for multiple sessions to consolidate.

findings.claim: Rapid gains from brief practice are stimulus-specific and do NOT generalize; only sustained multi-session training transfers to new, untrained material. This explains why genuine speed-listening improvement is a months-long arc rather than an instant skill, and matches the user's reported 4-month adaptation.
findings.evidence: Banai & Lavner (JASA 2014, PMID 25324090): brief exposure (20 sentences) and single-session groups improved on TRAINED material, but 'only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens.' Replicated by PMC3467231: 'Learning from brief exposure was specific and did not generalize.' Note: a standalone claim that '3 sessions are strictly required for any transfer' was REFUTED (1-2) as over-precise, but the directional finding (transfer needs sustained multi-session training) is well-supported.

findings.claim: Documented training outcome / realistic gain magnitude: after ~6 months of daily training, four of six participants improved from <14% to ~70% comprehension at 18 syll/sec. Critically, one of the four improvers was the study's NORMAL-SIGHTED control subject (reaching ~67% at 18 syll/sec), providing the single documented data point of a sighted listener trained toward the ultra-fast range.
findings.vote: claim 12, split 2-1 (numbers verified; participant label corrected)
findings.evidence: Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann (Front. Hum. Neurosci. 2013, PMC3805979): ~6 months, baseline <14%, 'four subjects showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points' at 18 syll/sec. IMPORTANT CORRECTION from verification: the study had five blind subjects + one sighted control (six total); subject 142 among the four improvers was the SIGHTED control (~67% at 18 syll/sec). So a sighted person reaching ~18 syll/sec with synthetic TTS is documented once — but it required ~6 months of intensive daily training and used formant-synthesized speech, not natural narration. The earlier-circulated claim that this sighted subject hit a hard ceiling and could not improve further was REFUTED (0-3).

findings.claim: Highest-leverage throughput trick that raises information without raising syllable rate: insert strategic silent pauses at clause and sentence boundaries ('time-restoration'). This restored normal-hearing young adults' recall to ~95% of normal-rate baseline (statistically indistinguishable, p=.874) even though words stayed at the fast compressed syllable rate, and reduced measured listening effort (pupil dilation).
findings.evidence: O'Leary et al., Trends in Hearing 2023 (DOI 10.1177/23312165231203514, PMC10637151): time-restoration 'raised recall accuracy... 95% of the way back to baseline... did not differ significantly (p=.874).' Mechanism is cognitive (resource-limited), not perceptual: fast speech 'deprive[s] the listener of ordinarily available processing time'; pupillometry showed reduced effort with restoration. Optimal scheme: silence ONLY at linguistic boundaries (not uniform), sentence-boundary pauses = 2x clause-boundary pauses, total added silence calibrated so the restored passage matches the original uncompressed duration. Caveat: the ~95%/p=.874 figure is the clear-speech condition; benefit weakened under degraded (vocoded) audio. This is the single most actionable lever for the user: keep syllable rate high but reinsert boundary pauses to let comprehension 'catch up.'

findings.claim: Second throughput mechanism (theta-band 'repackaging'): heavily time-compressed speech (3x) drops below 50% words-correct, but inserting periodic ~80 ms silent gaps restores intelligibility, following a U-shaped error curve. Periodic insertion beats aperiodic; the optimum re-establishes a ~9 Hz (high-theta) syllabic packaging rate that drains/re-syncs the perceptual buffer.
findings.evidence: Ghitza & Greenberg (Phonetica 2009, PMID 19390234): 3x compression <50% words correct; 'lowest word error rate occurred when the silence was 80 ms long and inserted periodically'; U-shaped curve, intelligibility restored for gaps 20-120 ms; aperiodic insertion raised error. Caveat: the exact 80 ms optimum is specific to the TTS + 40-ms-fragment paradigm (Ghitza 2014 got 96% at 3x with natural syllabic segmentation), so it is not a universal 'insert 80 ms anywhere' recipe — but the gap-insertion/repackaging mechanism (restore a ~theta-band packaging rate) is robust. A specific claim about 100 ms inter-syllable gaps helping across all spectral conditions was REFUTED (1-2).

findings.claim: Caution on the buffer-drain/pause mechanism: increased neural (cortical envelope) tracking from inserted pauses does NOT by itself prove improved comprehension, and aggressive pause insertion can REDUCE intelligibility. The behavioral benefit (the time-restoration recall result above) is the reliable evidence, not neural-tracking metrics.
findings.evidence: Deoisres et al. (PLOS ONE 2023, DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0289288): 'Whether the increased cortical response... reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation'; aggressive insertion (166 ms every 333 ms) reduced intelligibility. Implication: pause-insertion helps via giving cognitive catch-up time at the RIGHT (boundary) locations and durations, but over-fragmenting hurts — tune conservatively.

findings.claim: Content-aware variable-rate playback (normalizing all speech to a constant target syllable rate) is a real, deployed technique but is NOT a high-multiplier solution. The 'speech-speed' Chrome extension speeds slow speakers more and fast speakers less to hit a default 9 syll/sec target, yet caps at 3.5x default / 5x max — below the user's 7x-10x goals and only modestly above Audible's 3.5x.
findings.evidence: github.com/ywong137/speech-speed README: estimates speaker syllable rate via 2-10 Hz envelope modulation and normalizes to 'default 9 syl/s. Slow speakers get sped up more; fast speakers get sped up less'; 'Max speed (1.5-5x, default 3.5).' Confirms the right strategy (target a constant comfortable syllable rate rather than a blind multiplier — fast narrators don't need 3.5x, slow ones can take more) but also confirms no off-the-shelf consumer tool delivers 7x-10x because such rates exceed human comprehension regardless of player capability.

input.caveats: TOOLS COVERAGE IS THIN: the verified claim set robustly covers the science (sighted ceiling, training protocols, gap/pause throughput tricks) but the only concrete tool documented is the speech-speed Chrome extension (caps at 5x). The research did NOT surface verified evidence on custom players that exceed Audible's 3.5x via Rubber Band/SoundTouch re-encoding, on screen-reader TTS rates achievable in practice, or on community/practitioner (blind power-user, r/speedlistening) knowledge — claims 4 and 5 of the original brief are under-evidenced here and should be treated as open. BASELINE ARITHMETIC: 'x' translations depend on the assumed baseline. Audible '1x' ~150 wpm slow narration ~= ~3.5-3.9 syll/sec (English averages ~1.4 syll/word); normal conversation is ~4-6 syll/sec / ~180-220 wpm. So the user's 3.5x ~= ~13 syll/sec ~= ~525 wpm against the slow baseline but only ~2.5-3x of conversational rate — the '3.5x' headline overstates how far above normal human speech the user actually is. SYNTHETIC vs NATURAL: the headline 22 syll/sec ceiling is a formant-TTS, trained-blind result and does not transfer to compressed natural audiobooks; the realistic natural-speech sighted target is roughly 15-18 syll/sec at degraded comprehension. FIGURE VARIANCE: popular press (Scientific American) cites 25 vs 10 syll/sec; academic literature uses 22 vs 8 — same order of magnitude. EFFECT-SIZE FRAMING: the d=1.37 figure is a pre/post threshold difference, not a wpm or recall gain, and several training studies measured thresholds/verification rather than naturalistic comprehension. TIME-SENSITIVITY: the psychoacoustics is stable (2007-2023, replicated); the tool landscape is fast-moving (the extension is dated 2026) and should be re-checked.
openQuestions: What custom players or re-encoding pipelines (Rubber Band, SoundTouch, ffmpeg atempo + silence-trimming, or screen-reader TTS like NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver) actually let a sighted user combine >3.5x rate with automatic silence-trimming and boundary-pause re-insertion in one workflow? This was not verified.

Does the time-restoration / boundary-pause benefit (validated for one-shot narrative recall in normal-hearing adults) hold up over sustained multi-hour audiobook listening, and does it net positive on total throughput once the re-inserted silence is counted (it lengthens playback time even as it raises comprehension per word)?

Is there documented evidence of any SIGHTED listener exceeding ~18 syll/sec with NATURAL compressed speech (not synthetic TTS), and what comprehension level is sustainable there versus the ~70% the trained sighted control reached with synthetic TTS at 18 syll/sec?

What do experienced blind screen-reader power users and speed-listening enthusiasts report as practical sustainable rates and training tricks (e.g., switching to formant TTS, voice selection, gradual rate ramping in a screen reader) — the practitioner/community evidence layer was not captured in the verified claims?

content.text:
I've synthesized the 20 verified claims into 10 coherent findings answering all five parts of the research question. The structured report is complete.

The core answer: 7x and 10x as raw syllable-rate multipliers are not physically achievable for any human. The user's current 3.5x (~13 syll/sec) is already at the top of the untrained-sighted band, and even the absolute documented ceiling (~22 syll/sec, trained blind, synthetic TTS only) is only ~6x of the slow Audible baseline. The realistic path forward is progressive-ramp perceptual training (to push toward ~15-18 syll/sec) plus throughput tricks that raise information density without raising syllable rate — chiefly silence-trimming and strategic boundary-pause insertion ("time-restoration," which restored ~95% of normal recall while keeping words compressed). I flagged that the tools and practitioner-community layers of the brief were under-evidenced in the verified claim set.
